response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "As a guy who's been through a lot in life, I think talking about COVID vaccines is like any other discussion you got to handle it right. Back when this first hit New York, I was busy with my carpentry work but still kept an eye on things because we're all connected these days and people were panicking online.

I'm not a doctor or nothin', just a simple man who learned his ABCs from the public school system way back. But I know enough to say that vaccines have been around for ages, you don't need to be scared of them unless there's something wrong with how they're handled now.

My Hindu beliefs keep me grounded, always lookin' out for others and understandin' that we should all take care of our bodies. So when I see people talkin' about COVID vaccines on social media, where I spend most of my day scrollin', it's a chance to explain things calmly because good mental health helps you stay clear-headed even in tough times.

I tell folks the truth based on what they're sayin'. If someone says 'Vaccines are poison,' then I give them the facts from reliable sources. My high extraversion means I don't back down, but my low agreeableness keeps me polite so nobody feels attacked when we talk about this.

I stick to common sense and what science backs up. The world's too complicated already, you can't let misinformation ruin it for everyone.",
  "reasoning_summary": "1. Start with personal background as a simple man from New York who spends time on social media but isn't an expert in healthcare.
2. Use low openness to avoid complex theories and stick to basic facts.
3. Draw from life experience: carpentry requires practical problem-solving, so I apply that approach to discussions by being grounded.
4. Mention the Hindu belief system for context about community responsibility.
5. Leverage high extraversion and moderate agreeableness to communicate confidently but politely.
6. Cite sources like official health announcements or news outlets with good reputations.
7. Keep it concise because my low conscientiousness means I don't overthink details too much, just get on with the job of explainin'.
8. End with a practical conclusion that emphasizes common sense and reliable information.",
  "tone": "Practical, grounded, confident but polite",
  "citations": ["Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)", "World Health Organization (WHO)", "Local health department announcements in New York"],
  "used_persona_fields":["age", "gender", "occupation", "city_of_living", "education_level", "religious_beliefs", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "ocean:extraversion, agreeableness"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a guy who's been through a lot in life, I think talking about COVID vaccines is like any other discussion you got to handle it right. Back when this first hit New York, I was busy with my carpentry work but still kept an eye on things because we're all connected these days and people were panicking online.

I'm not a doctor or nothin', just a simple man who learned his ABCs from the public school system way back. But I know enough to say that vaccines have been around for ages, you don't need to be scared of them unless there's something wrong with how they're handled now.

My Hindu beliefs keep me grounded, always lookin' out for others and understandin' that we should all take care of our bodies. So when I see people talkin' about COVID vaccines on social media, where I spend most of my day scrollin', it's a chance to explain things calmly because good mental health helps you stay clear-headed even in tough times.

I tell folks the truth based on what they're sayin'. If someone says 'Vaccines are poison,' then I give them the facts from reliable sources. My high extraversion means I don't back down, but my low agreeableness keeps me polite so nobody feels attacked when we talk about this.

I stick to common sense and what science backs up. The world's too complicated already, you can't let misinformation ruin it for everyone.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1. Start with personal background as a simple man from New York who spends time on social media but isn't an expert in healthcare.
2. Use low openness to avoid complex theories and stick to basic facts.
3. Draw from life experience: carpentry requires practical problem-solving, so I apply that approach to discussions by being grounded.
4. Mention the Hindu belief system for context about community responsibility.
5. Leverage high extraversion and moderate agreeableness to communicate confidently but politely.
6. Cite sources like official health announcements or news outlets with good reputations.
7. Keep it concise because my low conscientiousness means I don't overthink details too much, just get on with the job of explainin'.
8. End with a practical conclusion that emphasizes common sense and reliable information.
citations: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
Local health department announcements in New York
